title: The Three Price Drops the White House Cited vs. The One That Matters
narrative: White House spokesperson Kush Desai stated: "The American economy remains on a solid trajectory... March CPI showed cooling core inflation and prices of beef, dairy, eggs, and prescription drugs actually declining thanks to the President's policies." Trump predicted on May 12 that inflation would drop as soon as the Iran war ends. The public narrative: the war's economic damage is contained and temporary, with the administration crediting its own policies for cooling prices.
telemetry: Brent crude up 44% since the war began; now trading at ~$105/barrel
CPI reached 3.3% — highest since May 2024; PCE projected at 4% by year-end (double the Fed's 2% target)
Dallas Fed research: war could add 0.2 to 1.8 percentage points to Q4/Q4 headline inflation in 2026
EY-Parthenon projects GDP drag of 0.3 percentage points, cutting growth to 1.8% vs. 2.1% in 2025
IEA predicts global natural gas supplies will remain tight for two years due to the conflict
Mark Zandi (Moody's): "The damage has already been done, in part because there's no going back on oil prices, at least not any time in the near future"
Desai cited beef, dairy, eggs (deflating) while ignoring energy — the exact sector driving the entire CPI surge
Gas expected to settle at $3.50 at best by year-end — still above pre-war levels
analysis: The White House found deflation in beef, dairy, and eggs. It ignored Brent crude up 44%, gasoline at $4.06 a gallon, and a Fed inflation gauge tracking toward 4%. That's not an economic policy achievement — it's a selective data deployment. Energy is the transmission mechanism between the Hormuz conflict and consumer prices. It's also the category the White House chose not to mention. The Dallas Fed's range of 0.2 to 1.8 percentage points of additional inflation from the war is itself a range that assumes the war continues; a permanent conflict scenario sits at the high end. Mark Zandi's observation — the damage is already done because oil prices don't reverse quickly — is the verdict the administration is trying to outrun. Growth projections cut from 2.1% to 1.8% are a real cost in lost output, not a statistical artifact. The administration is not wrong that some prices are deflating. It is wrong about which ones drive the headline number, and wrong about whether its own policies caused the deflations it cites.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The White House cherry-picked three food categories from an inflation report dominated by an energy shock it caused and has not resolved. Brent crude up 44%, gas at $4.06, and a Fed gauge tracking toward 4% are the numbers that matter for the actual inflation experience of actual consumers. The three prices the administration cited were already falling before the Iran war and would have fallen regardless. The energy-driven surge — the one that explains why Americans are paying more at the pump than they were in February — is the number the White House omitted. That's not a messaging problem. That's a narrative built on a selective reading of the data.
